coredata.dc:title: Quantitative removal of Hg(II) as Hg(0) using carbon cloths coated graphene quantum dots and their silver nanoparticles composite and application of Hg(0) for the sensitive determination of nitrobenzene
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Kappen, Jincymol
Aravind, Manikka Kubendran
Varalakshmi, Perumal
Ashokkumar, Balasubramaniem
John, S. Abraham
coredata.dc:description: The removal of Hg(II) by adsorption using various carbon based materials is extensively studied in the past. But, the disposal of the resulting Hg(II) adsorbed materials is the major concern. Herein, a simple and novel sustainable strategy is introduced to remediate Hg(II) as Hg(0) with the help of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) coated carbon cloth (CC) and utilize the formed Hg(0) as an sensor for nitrobenzene (NB). When GQDs coated CC (CC/GQDs) was contacted with Hg(II) of different concentration it was converted into Hg(0), which is confirmed by TEM and SEM. The reduction of Hg(II) to Hg(0) by CC/GQDs is further confirmed from the XPS spectrum obtained for Hg 4 f region at binding energies of 100 and 104 eV besides differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The Hg(II) removed from the solution is quantified by ICP-AES. It is found that CC/GQDs completely removed 10 ppm Hg(II) within 2 h. Further, to enhance the removal efficiency, silver nanoparticles are introduced on GQDs (Ag@GQDs). The CC/Ag@GQDs show 3-fold higher removal efficiency than CC/GQDs. Finally, the formed product, Hg(0) is successfully used as a sensitive electrochemical sensor for NB with the limit of detection of 30 pM (S/N = 3).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots coated carbon cloth
Ag@GQDs
Remediation of Hg(II)
Hg(0) electrocatalyst
Reduction of nitrobenzene

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs and GQDs capped silver nanoparticles
Procedure for the modification of carbon cloth (CC) with GQDs and Ag@GQDs
Characterization by SEM, TEM and EDAX
Characterization by SEM, TEM and EDAX
Mechanism for the removal of Hg(II) as Hg(0) by CC/GQDs and CC/Ag@GQDs
Selectivity and application in real samples
Electrocatalytic applications of Hg Coated GC towards the reduction of NB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) CC and CC/GQDs (B, C) before and (D-F) after immersed in 200 ppm Hg(II) for 1 h.
TEM images of (A–C) and (D) SAED pattern of Hg(0) formed on GQDs modified on carbon coated copper grid after immersed in 200 ppm Hg(II) for 15 min.
XPS of CC/GQDs after immersed in 200 ppm Hg(II) for 1 h: (A) survey spectrum and (B) deconvoluted narrow spectrum of Hg 4 f region.

DPVs recorded for (a) dissolution of 10 ppm Hg(II) electrodeposited on SPE, (b) dissolution of electrodeposited Hg(II) after soaked CC/GQDs in 10 ppm Hg(II) for (b) 30 min and (c) 1 (d) 1.5 and (e) 2 h in 0.1 M HClO4 at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.
SEM images of CC/Ag@GQDs (A) before and (B,C) after immersed in 200 ppm Hg(II) for 1 h.
XPS recorded for CC/Ag@GQDs after immersed in 200 ppm Hg(II) for 1 h: deconvoluted narrow spectra of (A) Hg 4 f and (B) Ag 3d regions.

(A) CVs recorded for the reduction of 0.5 mM NB at (a) bare GC, (b) GC/GQDs, (c) GC/GQDs-Hg-a, (d) GC/GQDs-Hg-b (e) GC/GQDs-Hg-c (f) in the absence of NB at GC/GQDs-Hg-c in 0.2 M PBS at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

DPVs obtained for (A) each increment of 5 µM NB (a–j) and (B) wide range (500 nM-1 mM) addition of NB at GC/GQDs-Hg-c in 0.2 M PBS (pH 7.0) at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. Insets: A plot of current vs. concentration of NB; Expanded view for NB reduction from 500 nM to 10 μM.
Scheme showing the modification of CC with GQDs and Ag@GQDs.
ICP-AES data showing the removal of Hg(II) by using CC/GQDs.
ICP-AES data showing the removal of Hg(II) by using CC/Ag@GQDs.

head.ce:title: Quantitative removal of Hg(II) as Hg(0) using carbon cloths coated graphene quantum dots and their silver nanoparticles composite and application of Hg(0) for the sensitive determination of nitrobenzene

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram-624 302, Dindigul, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram-624 302

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram-624 302, Dindigul, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biotechnology, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biotechnology, Madurai Kamaraj University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Genetic Engineering, School of Biotechnology, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The removal of Hg(II) by adsorption using various carbon based materials is extensively studied in the past. But, the disposal of the resulting Hg(II) adsorbed materials is the major concern. Herein, a simple and novel sustainable strategy is introduced to remediate Hg(II) as Hg(0) with the help of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) coated carbon cloth (CC) and utilize the formed Hg(0) as an sensor for nitrobenzene (NB). When GQDs coated CC (CC/GQDs) was contacted with Hg(II) of different concentration it was converted into Hg(0), which is confirmed by TEM and SEM. The reduction of Hg(II) to Hg(0) by CC/GQDs is further confirmed from the XPS spectrum obtained for Hg 4 f region at binding energies of 100 and 104 eV besides differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The Hg(II) removed from the solution is quantified by ICP-AES. It is found that CC/GQDs completely removed 10 ppm Hg(II) within 2 h. Further, to enhance the removal efficiency, silver nanoparticles are introduced on GQDs (Ag@GQDs). The CC/Ag@GQDs show 3-fold higher removal efficiency than CC/GQDs. Finally, the formed product, Hg(0) is successfully used as a sensitive electrochemical sensor for NB with the limit of detection of 30 pM (S/N = 3).

The present study describes the development of sustainable method to remediate Hg(II) as Hg(0) by using graphene quantum dots coated carbon cloth (CC/GQDs) and utilization of Hg(0) as a sensitive electrochemical sensor for nitrobenzene
ga1

ce:keyword.ce:text: Graphene quantum dots coated carbon cloth

ce:section.ce:para: Remediation of mercuric ion (Hg(II)) from water resources is important because of its acute toxicity to human health and aquatic environment [1]. Hg exists in other forms including Hg(I) and Hg(0). Among the different forms, Hg(II) is the most reactive and can easily form the most dangerous compound methyl mercury (CH3Hg+) [2]. Various industries such as oil refineries, plastic, mining, cement and the disposal from fluorescent lamps can increase the level of Hg(0) in atmosphere [3]. In addition, natural processes like anthropogenic activities and volcanic eruption also release Hg(0) [4]. Even though developed and few developing countries banned Hg(0) involved appliances, its leaching from anthropogenic sources cannot be stopped. The Hg(0) and its oxides and sulfides disposed into the aquatic sources can be easily oxidized by microorganisms and entered into the aquatic species. The most common human exposure to mercury includes the inhalation of Hg(0) vapor from industries, dental amalgams and ingestion of Hg contaminated fish [2]. The ill-effects of bio-accumulated Hg were dictated by its extend of exposure. The bio-accumulated Hg can easily bind with amino acids having thiol groups thereby preventing the protein synthesis in the body [1,5]. Further, Hg contamination mainly affects the central nervous system, kidney, DNA methylation and cardiovascular system [6]. Owing to these health related issues, world health organization (WHO) fixed the permissible level of Hg(II) in drinking water as 2 ppb [7]. Therefore, it is vital to remediate Hg(II) from water sources permanently with simple and low cost methods.

Conventional methods such as ion exchange, chemical precipitation, adsorption and membrane flotation are generally used in the past for the removal of Hg(II) [8]. Among these techniques, adsorption was considered as the most popular technique because other techniques are having high cost, poor selectivity and maintenance issues [9]. In the literature, several thousands of papers are available for the removal of Hg(II) by adsorption technique and it is difficult to cite all the papers here. To mention a few, activated carbon and its composites [10,11], chitosan and its derivatives [12], cellulose [13], functionalized polymers [14,15], mesoporous carbon [16], zeolites [17], metal organic frameworks [18], graphene based materials [19,20] and different carbon based waste materials have been used as adsorbents for the removal of Hg(II) [21,22]. Functionalization by thiol groups are studied in the past to improve the adsorption capacity of adsorbent because Hg(II) has high affinity with thiol groups [14,15,23]. Until now, adsorption method faces sort of troubles including the stability of material, low surface area and unequal distribution of functional groups [21]. Very importantly, no viable method is available for the disposal of Hg(II) adsorbed materials. Generally, the adsorbent loaded with Hg(II) may be dumped into waste lands, rivers and ponds after adsorption. These dumped materials may leach Hg(II) again into the water/soil bodies. Thus, adsorption is not a reliable method for the remediation of Hg(II).

Amalgamation with metal nanoparticles is an another approach of removing Hg(II) which includes silver (Ag) [24,25], gold (Au) [26], copper (Cu) [27], platinum (Pt) [28] and iron (Fe0, Fe-oxide) [29–31]. Again, the disposal of the amalgamated product was not addressed in the reported papers similar to the disposal of Hg(II) adsorbed materials. For the sustainable development, it is highly desirable that the Hg(II) adsorbed material and amalgam would have utilized for useful applications. Taking this issue into account, the present study aims to remove Hg(II) as Hg(0) on one hand and effective utilization of the resulting Hg(0) for electrocatalytic applications on the other hand. Here, carbon cloth (CC) coated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and their composite with AgNPs were effectively used as platforms for the removal of Hg(II) as Hg(0) and utilize the resulting Hg(0) as a sensitive sensor for nitrobenzene (NB). The importance of metallic mercury is demonstrated since 1922 when Jaroslav Heyrovsky used it for the detection of more than dozen metal ions in a mixture by polarography [32]. The GQDs used in the present study were prepared from simple bottom up approach using citric acid as a precursor and then they were coated on carbon cloth (CC/GQDs) to remove Hg(II) by immersing it into Hg(NO3)2 dissolved in water. The spectroscopic and microscopic techniques confirm the presence of metallic mercury (Hg(0)) on GQDs. The removal of Hg(II) as Hg(0) was further studied with respect to contact time, Hg(II) concentration and the area of CC. The CC/GQDs with 1 cm2 size completely removed 10 ppm Hg(II) within 2 h. Further, the removal efficiency was also studied by incorporating silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) into GQDs (Ag@GQDs) under identical conditions as adopted for CC/GQDs. The removal efficiency of CC/Ag@GQDs towards Hg(II) was 3-fold higher than that of CC/GQDs. Finally, the product Hg(0) formed on GQDs was successfully utilized as a sensitive sensor for NB with the limit of detection (LOD) of 30 pM (S/N = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs and GQDs capped silver nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysing citric acid (CA) in accordance with our recent paper [33]. The as synthesized GQDs were used for the synthesis of Ag@GQDs [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the modification of carbon cloth (CC) with GQDs and Ag@GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The modification of CC with GQDs (CC/GQDs) and Ag@GQDs (CC/Ag@GQDs) is illustrated in Scheme 1. Both of them were coated on CC separately by drop casting to cover the entire surface. After drying at room temperature, Hg(II) removal was studied by soaking CC/GQDs and CC/Ag@GQDs in different concentrations of Hg(II) separately at various time intervals. Same procedure was adopted for the modification of GQDs and Ag@GQDs on GC substrate.
The as synthesized GQDs and Ag@GQDs were characterized by UV-vis, fluorescence, and XPS techniques and the details were given in the supporting information (Fig. S1 and S2).

Very recently, our research group studied the interaction of Hg(II) with GQDs and Ag@GQDs [35,36]. When the GQDs interact with Hg(II) in solution from 1 h to 7 days, the formation of Hg droplets with size ranging from 100 nm to 2 µm is noticed from the SEM images of GC plate coated with a mixture of GQDs and Hg(II) at the respective time intervals [35]. On the other hand, when Ag@GQDs modified surface is interacted with Hg(II), formation of flower like Hg(0) is noticed with the complete displacement of AgNPs by Hg(II) [36]. Even traces of Hg(I)/Hg(II) was not found on the surface of GQDs as evidenced from XPS studies. Nevertheless, the ultimate product is Hg(0) in both the cases. Taking this advantage, the present study mainly focuses to quantify the removal of Hg(II) by immersing GQDs and Ag@GQDs coated carbon cloth (CC) in different concentrations of Hg(II) by ICP-AES and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques. The following section highlights the removal of Hg(II) by using CC/GQDs and CC/Ag@GQDs under different experimental conditions. The application of formed Hg(0) as an electrocatalyst for HER and sensitive sensor for one of the organic pollutants, NB will be demonstrated in the subsequent Section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization by SEM, TEM and EDAX
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the SEM images of CC, CC/GQDs and CC/GQDs after immersed in 200 ppm Hg(II) for 1 h. The SEM image of CC shows the presence of fibers since it was made up of carbon fibers, which do not have smooth and flat surface (Fig. 1A). The presence of spherical particles confirms the successful coating of GQDs by drop casting (Fig. 1B and C). Further, the SEM images show that the surface of CC was completely covered by GQDs. The SEM images of CC/GQDs recorded at different areas of CC after soaking in 200 ppm Hg (II) shows the formation of Hg(0) (Fig. 1D–F). Even after scanning the entire CC surface, uniform formation of Hg(0) could not be identified. At certain places, Hg(0) was appeared as droplets with different sizes as shown in Fig. 1D. In addition, the formation of Hg(0) as clusters was also noticed at certain areas of CC/GQDs (Fig. 1E).

Moreover, as depicted in Fig. 1F, CC surface was completely covered by Hg(0) at certain areas where the edges of CC opposed the formation of Hg droplets and become joined and flattened due to surface tension. For comparison, the formation of Hg(0) was also studied by coating GQDs on GC plate followed by immersion in 200 ppm Hg(II) (Fig. S3). In contrast to CC (Fig. 1D–F), the formation of Hg(0) droplets with a size of 5 µm was noticed on GC plate (Fig. S3A). This is due to the smooth and uniform surface of GC when compared to CC. While scanning the entire GC surface, Hg(0) droplets with 3 and 10 µm size were noticed as depicted in Fig. S3B and C. Thus, Hg(0) with uniform size was not formed on GC surface. For the same concentration of Hg(II) in solution phase with GQDs, formation of Hg drops with the maximum size of 2 µm was observed after 1 h. This reveals that the reduction kinetics of Hg(II) to Hg(0) on GQDs coated CC surface were faster than in solution [35].

The TEM images recorded at different magnifications for GQDs coated TEM grid after immersed in 200 ppm Hg(II) for 15 min are shown in Fig. 2. The average size of Hg(0) was found to be 1 µm as evidenced from Fig. 2A. In addition, the formation of small Hg(0) droplet was also noticed from TEM images, indicating the presence of new nucleation sites of Hg(0). As illustrated in Fig. 2B and C, merging of two Hg(0) droplets was also noticed at different areas in addition to the presence of single Hg(0). The amorphous nature of Hg(0) was evidenced from SAED pattern (Fig. 2D). The presence of Hg(0) was further confirmed from EDAX spectra of CC/GQDs after soaked in 200 ppm Hg(II). The EDAX spectra show X-ray binding energy at 2.2 and 10 eV, respectively for Lα and Mα of Hg (Fig. S4). The presence of C and O from GQDs was confirmed at binding energies of 0.2 and 0.25 eV, respectively.

The removal of Hg(II) by CC/GQDs was further analyzed by recording the XPS of CC/GQDs after immersed in 200 ppm Hg(II) for 1 h ( Fig.3). As depicted in Fig.3A, the survey spectrum shows the presence of Hg, C and O with the binding energy (BE) of 100, 280 and 530 eV, respectively. The deconvoluted spectrum of Hg 4 f region shows a doublet at BE of 99.9 and 103.9 eV with the difference of 4 eV (Fig.3B), confirming the presence of Hg(0) [35–37]. In addition, the dissolution peaks observed at BE of 101.4 and 106.1 eV represents the presence of both Hg(I) and Hg(II) on GQDs.

The CC/GQDs was immersed in 200 ppm Hg(II) for 1 h and then it was washed with water to strip Hg(0) by DPV. The differential pulse voltammogram obtained for CC/GQDs in 0.1 M HClO4 in the potential range of 0.0–0.80 V is shown in Fig. S5. The oxidation peak observed at 0.48 V confirmed the dissolution of Hg(0) to Hg(II). In addition, the oxidation peak obtained at 0.68 V indicates the dissolution of adsorbed Hg(0) on the graphene surface of GQDs [35,37]. On the other hand, the dissolution peak obtained at 0.32 V was attributed to the oxidation of Hg(I).

The removal of Hg(II) by CC/GQDs was quantified by ICP-AES. Table 1 summarizes the ICP-AES data obtained for the removal of Hg(II) with respect to contact time, Hg(II) concentration and surface area of CC. First the removal of Hg(II) was studied by varying the contact time of CC/GQDs by keeping the CC area (1 cm2) and concentration of Hg(II) (20 ppm Hg(II)) constants. The CC was coated with GQDs by drop casting and allowed to dry at room temperature. Then, the respective CC/GQDs was immersed separately into 20 ppm Hg(II) for 1 and 2 h. After removing CC/GQDs from Hg(II), the solution was acid digested to remove impurities and then recorded ICP-AES. As shown in Tables 1, 1 h immersion of CC/GQDs (1 cm2) removed 4.2 ppm Hg(II) from 20 ppm whereas removal of 11.2 ppm was achieved after 2 h immersion. Further, the effective removal of Hg(II) was also studied by varying the concentration of Hg(II) by immersing CC/GQDs (1 cm2) separately into 10 and 50 ppm Hg(II) for 2 h. The ICP-AES results show that a removal of 10 and 27.3 ppm Hg(II), respectively was achieved. These results reveal that the rate of Hg(II) removal becomes faster while increasing the concentration of Hg(II). It is to be noted here that a complete removal of Hg(II) was achieved when CC/GQDs was in contact with 10 ppm Hg(II) for 2 h. Finally, the effect of surface area of CC (1, 2, and 3 cm2) towards Hg(II) removal was studied by keeping the immersion time of 1 h and 20 ppm Hg(II) as constants. As mentioned above, the removal of 4.2 ppm Hg(II) was noticed for 1 cm2 CC. On the other hand, a removal of 9.8 and 14 ppm Hg(II) was obtained while increasing the area of CC into 2 and 3 cm2, respectively. This indicates that the effective removal of Hg(II) increases with the increase in surface area of CC. The results obtained from ICP-AES demonstrate that the present strategy can be used for the successful remediation of bulk Hg(II) from water.

Further, an attempt is made to quantify Hg(II) removal by DPV and then compared with ICP-AES. Here, after the immersion of CC/GQDs (1 cm2) in Hg(II) at various time intervals, Hg(II) left out in the same solution was taken and then electrochemically deposited at a constant potential followed by stripping. Based on the dissolution current of the electrochemically deposited Hg(0), Hg(II) remaining in the solution was quantified. The Hg(II) concentration was restricted to 10 ppm. The electrodeposition was carried out on screen printed electrode (SPE) by applying a constant potential of − 0.1 V for 100 s in 0.1 M HClO4. After the deposition of Hg(0), the stripping was done in the potential range of 0.0–0.80 V in 0.1 M HClO4. Fig.4 shows the stripping of electrochemically deposited Hg(II) left out in the solution after the immersion of CC/GQDs at various time intervals. First, 10 ppm Hg(II) was deposited and then its dissolution was recorded by DPV. The dissolution of deposited Hg(0) occurs at 0.44 V ( Fig.4 , curve a). Then, the CC/GQDs was soaked in 10 ppm Hg(II) with the contact time of 30 min and subsequent electrodeposition of Hg(II) left out in the solution followed by its dissolution was recorded. Here, the dissolution current was decreased from 3 to 2 μA at the same potential (curve b), indicating CC/GQDs successfully removed Hg(II). The dissolution current was further decreased to 1.4 and 1 μA, respectively after 1 h and 1.5 h (curves c and d) immersion of CC/GQDs in 10 ppm Hg(II). After 2 h immersion of CC/GQDs, no appreciable electrochemical response was obtained due to complete removal of Hg(II) (curve e). This is in accordance with the ICP-AES data where CC/GQDs (1 cm2) completely removed 10 ppm Hg(II) with the contact time of 2 h. Thus, the DPV can also be used to quantify the removal of Hg(II).

Very recently, we found that when GC plate coated with Ag@GQDs is incubated in 200 ppm Hg(II) for 15 min, AgNPs are completely replaced by Hg(II), in which the SEM image shows the formation of Hg(0) with flower like morphology on GC plate [36]This idea was extended to remove Hg(II) under identical conditions as adopted for CC/GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization by SEM, TEM and EDAX
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM, TEM, XPS and DPV studies were carried out by immersing CC/Ag@GQDs in 200 ppm Hg(II) for 1 h. The SEM images of CC/Ag@GQDs before and after immersed in Hg(II) for 1 h are depicted in Fig. 5. A uniform coverage of Ag@GQDs on CC was noticed from Fig. 5A. The SEM images of CC/Ag@GQDs after immersed in Hg(II) for 1 h shows the formation of Hg(0) on CC. Similar results were also obtained from the TEM studies. Fig. S6A shows the TEM image of Ag@GQDs after treated with Hg(II). It shows the presence of Hg(0) and the corresponding SAED pattern confirmed its amorphous nature (Fig. S6B). The formation of Hg(0) was also supported by the EDAX spectrum (Fig. S6C).

Fig.6 shows the XPS of CC/Ag@GQDs after soaked in Hg(II) for 1 h. The deconvoluted narrow spectra of both Hg 4 f and Ag 3d regions are given in Fig.6A and B. The presence of a doublet at BE of 100 and 104 eV indicates the reduction of Hg(II) to Hg(0), facilitated by electron rich GQDs and the galvanic reaction (GR) with AgNPs (Fig.6A) [36]. As a result of Hg(0) deposition, AgNPs from the surface of GQDs undergo dissolution as Ag+ leading to a decrement in the atomic percentage of Ag(0) from 0.5 to < 0.2% in contrast to untreated Ag@GQDs (Fig.6B). On comparison with CC/GQDs, the atomic percentage of Hg(0) was increased from 0.1 to 0.3 at Ag@GQDs. Thus, the presence of AgNPs on GQDs surface shows better removal efficiency of Hg(II) as Hg(0) when compared to GQDs alone.

Additionally, the formation of Hg(0) on GC/Ag@GQDs was confirmed by DPV. The differential pulse voltammograms of GC/Ag@GQDs after contacted with Hg(II) for 1 h are illustrated in Fig. S7. Instead of CC, GC electrode was used here. As exhibited in Fig. S7, the oxidation peak was observed at 0.48 V for GC/GQDs due to the dissolution of Hg(0) as Hg(II) (curve a). On the other hand, the dissolution peak was appeared at 0.45 V for GC/Ag@GQDs (curve b) with 3-fold higher oxidation current than that of GC/GQDs. In both the cases, the dissolution peak locating around 0.65 V was due to the oxidation of strongly adsorbed Hg(0) on the graphene moiety of GQDs [35,37].

As in the case of CC/GQDs, here also the effect of contact time, Hg(II) concentration and surface area of CC on the removal of Hg(II) was studied ( Table 2). Initially, the contact time of CC/Ag@GQDs on Hg(II) was varied as 1 and 2 h by keeping the surface area (1 cm2) and Hg(II) concentration (20 ppm) constant. After 1 h immersion, 13.4 ppm Hg(II) was removed by CC/Ag@GQDs whereas a complete removal of 20 ppm was achieved after 2 h. In contrast to CC/GQDs, removal of Hg(II) by CC/Ag@GQDs was 3-fold higher. Moreover, the removal of Hg(II) by CC/Ag@GQDs was increased while increasing Hg(II) concentration because of the fast reduction kinetics. For example, 1 h immersion of CC/Ag@GQDs (1 cm2) in 50 ppm Hg(II) removed 40.8 ppm Hg(II) from the solution but it was only 13.4 ppm while immersed in 20 ppm Hg(II). Thus, the conversion of Hg(II) to Hg(0) was more facile at both CC/GQDs and CC/Ag@GQDs while increasing Hg(II) concentration. Finally, the effect of surface area of CC on the removal of Hg(II) was studied. The immersion of CC/Ag@GQDs with an area of 1 cm2 was able to remove 13.4 ppm Hg(II) while a complete removal of 20 ppm was achieved when the surface area of CC was increased to 2 cm2. Overall, the removal efficiency of CC/Ag@GQDs was found to be 3-fold higher than that of CC/GQDs as evidenced from DPV and ICP-AES measurements. In any case, both CC/GQDs and CC/Ag@GQDs can be used as an efficient platform for the removal of not only traces but also high concentration of Hg(II) [38]. Even though, the removal efficiency of Hg(II) by CC/Ag@GQDs was higher than CC/GQDs, it has two important demerits which include (i) the precursor used for the synthesis of AgNPs is expensive and (ii) contamination of water due to the dissolution of Ag(I). Considering these two serious issues, the strategy of removing Hg(II) with the use of CC/GQDs is the best platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the removal of Hg(II) as Hg(0) by CC/GQDs and CC/Ag@GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The reduction of Hg(II) by CC/GQDs is expected to follow polyol mechanism since the GQDs prepared from CA enriched with hydroxyl groups. Upon the reduction of Hg(II), the hydroxyl groups were oxidized to carbonyl groups. The resultant Hg(0) was capable of adsorbing on the graphene moiety of GQDs since it’s a heavy metal [35]. In the case of Ag@GQDs, the GR between Ag(0) and Hg(II) leads to the formation of Hg(0) on the surface of GQDs and Ag(0) leaches out into the solution as Ag(I) [36]. Here, the presence of GQDs surface prevents the formation of Ag-Hg amalgam and finally Hg(0) adsorbs on the graphene surface of GQDs.

The effect of pH and temperature on the removal of CC/GQDs and CC/Ag@GQDs were also studied. Dilute nitric acid was used to adjust the pH of GQDs from 13 to 7 and 5. Among the different pH, pH 13 was found to be the most effective for the reduction of Hg(II) to Hg(0). At the same time, the complexation of Hg(II) was predominant at pH 7. Thus, immediate removal of Hg(II) by complexation was possible at pH 7 while the removal of Hg(II) as Hg(0) was effective at pH 13. At the same time, the removal of Hg(II) as Hg(0) by CC/Ag@GQDs was found to be less at acidic pH due to their aggregation. The removal efficiency of Hg(II) by both GQDs and Ag@GQDs was found to be decreased while increasing the temperature. This was ascribed to the aggregation of both GQDs [37] and Ag@GQDs.

The application of CC/GQDs for the removal of other mercury species like Hg(I) was also studied. The removal of Hg(I) to Hg(0) was followed by CV. The CC/GQDs with an area of 1 cm2 was immersed in 20 ppm Hg(I) solution for 1 h. The removal of Hg(I) as Hg(0) was initially confirmed by stripping the Hg species on CC as given in Fig. S8. The oxidation peak observed at 0.33 V (curve a) indicates the presence of Hg(I) on the surface of GQDs, which may be in the form of complexed or adsorbed state. On the other hand, the oxidation peak obtained at 0.46 V (curve b) clearly shows the formation of Hg(0) on the surface of GQDs. Further, the obtained oxidation current of 1.3 mA implies the bulk removal of Hg(I) as Hg(0). Here, the stripping current was 3.2-fold higher than that obtained for 20 ppm Hg(II) under identical experimental conditions. This indicates that the reduction efficiency of Hg(I) to Hg(0) was higher than that of Hg(II) to Hg(0). Besides, the time required for the complete removal of Hg(I) to Hg(0) was faster than Hg(II).
The adsorption efficiency of CC/GQDs and CC/Ag@GQDs was calculated by using the following equation.
Adsorption (removal) efficiency (%) = [(C0 - C)/C0] × 100.

where C0 is the initial concentration of Hg(II) and C is the final concentration of Hg(II). The adsorption efficiency of CC/GQDs and CC/Ag@GQDs vary with respect to its cross sectional area, concentration of Hg(II) and immersion time. For example, the adsorption efficiency of CC/GQDs with 1 cm2 area for 1 h immersion in 20 ppm Hg(II) was estimated as 21% while it was increased to 49% for 2 cm2. The adsorption efficiency of CC/Ag@GQDs was found to be 67% for 1 cm2 after 1 h immersion in 20 ppm. Meanwhile, it was 100% after 2 h immersion.

To check the reusability of CC/GQDs and CC/Ag@GQDs, the adsorbed Hg(0) was stripped by applying the potential and the CC was again coated by drop casting GQDs/ Ag@GQDs and it was again used for the removal of Hg(II). The removal efficiency was retained for five consecutive measurements and it was decreased slightly for further measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and application in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Further, the selectivity of CC/GQDs was monitored by immersing 20 ppm Hg(II) containing 5-fold higher concentration of Cu(II), Cd(II), Ni(II), Mn(II), Co(III), Fe(III), Fe(II), Pb(II), Cr(III) and Zn(II). After 1 h immersion of CC/GQDs into this mixture, it was found that 4 ppm Hg(II) was removed from the solution which reveals that even in the presence of higher concentration of metal ions CC/GQDs selectively reduced Hg(II) to Hg(0). Even though the interference from Fe(III) was reported earlier [37] in the selective sensing of Hg(II) in solution, it was not observed in the present study since it follows surface reactions besides the interaction of Fe(III) with GQDs is pH dependent.

To check the applicability of the present materials in real sample, similar experiments were also executed by immersing CC/GQDs in lake, bore, tap, river and sea water samples. Since Hg(II) was absent in the collected samples, 20 and 10 ppm of Hg(II) were spiked into the corresponding water samples. Then, CC/GQDs was immersed for 1 h and quantified the removal of Hg(II) by ICP-AES. The removal efficiency of 24%, 18%, 20%, 48% and 11% Hg(II) was estimated for lake, bore, tap, river and sea waters, respectively (Table S1). Among the different water samples, the removal efficiency was least for sea water sample under identical experimental conditions. This may be due to the formation of stable complexes of Hg(II) with Cl- ions enriched sea water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic applications of Hg Coated GC towards the reduction of NB
ce:section.ce:para: The other objective of the present investigation is to employ the deposited Hg(0) for the detection of one of the organic pollutants, nitrobenzene. Although the present strategy of removing highly toxic Hg(II) with the aid of GQDs is interesting the formed elemental mercury, Hg(0) is also toxic. Therefore, it must be either converted into non-toxic form or utilized for suitable applications. The use of Hg electrode directly for the voltammetric determination of reactive oxygen species, cysteine, cystine and albumin at Hg electrode has been demonstrated for more than five decades ago by Kolthoff and his-workers [39]. Very recently, Harika et al. prepared solid Hg nanoparticles (HgNPs) at room temperature with the aid of sonication using liquid Hg and rGO and studied the electrocatalytic activity of Hg solid nanoparticles towards HER and zinc deposition/dissolution [40]. They have shown that HgNPs modified GC electrode showed higher electrocatalytic activity than Hg drop electrode. Keeping the aforementioned applications of Hg in mind, the present study aims to exploit the deposited Hg(0) on GQDs as an electrocatalyst for the reduction of nitrobenzene (NB).

The Hg(0) deposited GC electrodes were prepared by immersing GQDs coated GC electrodes in Hg(II) and then used as an electrocatalyst. Since the removal of Hg(II) to Hg(0) was increased with increasing the concentration of Hg(II), it is expected that the electrocatalytic activity can also vary with the coverage and size of Hg(0). Therefore, SEM studies were first carried out to find the size and distribution of Hg(0) formed on GC/GQDs by immersing into 2, 20 and 200 ppm Hg(II) for 1 h. The resultant GC substrates were designated as Hg-a, Hg-b and Hg-c, respectively. Unlike CC, GC surface shows the formation of well-defined Hg(0) drops. As displayed in Fig. S9, the size of formed Hg(0) was 700 nm for Hg-a whereas 1.6 and 15 µm for Hg-b and Hg-c, respectively (Fig. S9 A-C). In the case of Hg-c, Hg drops with various sizes were noticed similar to the SEM images shown in Fig. S3A-C. While scanning the entire surface, one Hg droplet was identified with the maximum size of 15 µm. On comparison with Hg-a and Hg-b, population of Hg(0) on GC plate was higher for Hg-c. It is to be noted here that unlike gold and silver nanoparticles Hg(0) could not cover the entire surface. This is due to the joining of nearby small Hg(0) droplets to form a single large size droplet due to surface tension.

After SEM analysis, the electrochemical impedance studies were also carried out for Hg-a, Hg-b and Hg-c (Fig. S10) modified GC electrodes. As illustrated in Fig. S10a, the RCT value of 18 kΩ (curve a) was obtained for bare GC electrode and it was decreased to 15 kΩ after modified with GQDs (curve b). The RCT values of 2.3, 1.9 and 1.5 kΩ were obtained for Hg-a, Hg-b and Hg-c, respectively (curves c-e). The lower RCT value of Hg-c may be attributed to the presence of highly populated Hg(0) with large size on the surface of GQDs.

The application of Hg(0) deposited on the surface of GQDs was first examined for possible sensing of NB. Fig. 7. A shows the electrochemical reduction of 0.5 mM NB at GC, GC/GQDs, GC/GQDs-Hg electrodes in 0.2 M PB solution (pH 7.0). The reduction of NB occurs at − 0.77 V at bare GC electrode (curve a) whereas it was shifted to − 0.70 V at GC/GQDs electrode with 2.7-fold higher current than bare GC electrode (curve b). The obtained 70 mV less negative shift in the NB reduction potential was likely due to π-π interaction between GQDs and NB. No significant change in the reduction potential of NB was obtained between GC/GQDs-Hg-a, GC/GQDs-Hg-b and GC/GQDs-Hg-c electrodes. However, the overpotential of NB reduction was significantly decreased in the range of 120–130 and 60–80 mV at these electrodes when compared to bare GC and GC/GQDs, electrodes respectively. The reduction of NB occurs at − 0.62 V for Hg-a, − 0.63 V for Hg-b and − 0.64 V for Hg-c electrodes (curves c-e). Among the different Hg(0) electrodes, GC/GQDs-Hg-c shows the highest reduction current for NB. It shows more than 4-fold larger current than bare GC electrode (curve e). The obtained higher electrocatalytic activity of Hg-c was attributed to the presence of more number of Hg(0) particles with large size on the surface of GQDs. Besides, the synergistic effect GQDs-Hg composite could also favor the reduction of NB. In the absence of NB, GC/GQDs-Hg-c electrode shows no electrochemical response in the potential window studied (curve f).

The GC/Hg-c electrode was selected for the sensitive and selective determination of NB because of its higher electrocatalytic activity. Fig. 8A shows the DPVs obtained for the reduction of NB in the concentration range of 5–50 µM (each 5 µM addition) at GC/GQDs-Hg-c in 0.2 M PB solution (pH 7). After the addition of 5 µM NB (curve a), a reduction peak of NB occurs at − 0.50 V. The NB reduction current increases while increasing its concentration from 10 to 50 µM (curves b-j) without affecting the potential. The plot between peak current against NB concentration shows linearity with a correlation coefficient of 0.9967 (Fig. 8A inset). The determination of NB was also carried out in wide range concentration using GC/Hg-c. Fig. 8B displays the DPVs recorded for NB in the concentration range from 500 nM to 1 mM in 0.2 M PB solution (pH 7). The NB reduction current linearly increases against its concentration with R2 = 0.9943 (Fig. 8B inset) and the LOD was found to be 30 pM (S/N = 3) was achieved. Further, the selectivity of GC/GQDs-Hg-c was studied further by adding 100 µM NB and 500 µM each of benzene, aniline, tannic acid, biphenol and alkali metal ions. No change in the voltammetric response was observed after the addition of these compounds. But, the addition of equal concentration of nitrophenol altered the NB reduction current without affecting its potential. Therefore, selective determination of NB in the presence of nitrophenol was not possible using GC/GQDs-Hg. However, the determination of NB was possible in the presence of 5-fold higher concentration of other interferents. The catalytic activity of the GC/GQDs-Hg was compared with the other metallic nanoparticles which include Ag, Pt, Pd and Au nanoparticles [41–46] (Table S1). It can be seen from Table S2, GC/GQDs-Hg showed the lowest LOD and can be used to determine wide linear range of NB when compared to the reported papers. Very importantly, the sensor used in the present study is very cheaper in contrast to expensive Pt, Pd, Au and Pt nanoparticles.

To conclude, an efficient, cost effective and simple strategy for the removal of Hg(II) from water was introduced by drop casting GQDs and Ag@GQDs on carbon cloth followed by immersing them in an aqueous solution of Hg(II). It was fascinating to realize the formation of Hg(0) on CC after the removal of Hg(II). The hydroxyl functionalities were involved in the reduction of Hg(II) besides the electron rich graphitic surface of GQDs. In the case of CC/Ag@GQDs, the redox reaction between AgNPs and Hg(II) induced more deposition of Hg(0) on CC. The formation of Hg(0) was confirmed by SEM, TEM, XPS and DPV. The Hg(II) removal was quantified by ICP-AES and DPV measurements. Under optimized conditions, CC/GQDs with an area of 1 cm2 completely removed 10 ppm Hg(II) within 2 h. The effective removal of Hg(II) was increased while increasing the area of the CC to 2 and 3 cm2. The effect of contact time on the removal of Hg(II) was also studied and found that the removal of Hg(II) increases with increase in contact time. From ICP-AES data, the uptake of Hg(II) by CC/Ag@GQDs was 3-fold higher than CC/GQDs under identical experimental conditions. The application of the present platform for the removal of Hg(II) from bore, lake and tap water samples was also demonstrated. Finally, the usage of Hg(0) coated GC was demonstrated by studying its electrocatalytic activity towards NB reduction. The NB reduction was studied at GQDs-Hg electrodes prepared by immersing in different Hg(II) concentrations. Among 2, 20 and 200 ppm Hg(II), Hg(0) formation was highest from 200 ppm Hg(II) with various sized drops on GC surface. The maximum size of Hg(0) formed was 700 nm, 1.6 and 15 µm, respectively from 2, 20 and 200 ppm Hg(II). The GC/Hg electrodes not only decreased the overpotential of NB reduction by more than 100 mV but also enhanced the oxidation current for more than 4-fold when compared to bare GC electrode. As the concentration of NB increases, its reduction current was increased linearly with R2 = 0.9967 (S/N = 3) and the LOD of 30 pM (S/N = 3) was achieved. Overall, the present study proposes a sustainable method for the remediation of Hg(II) by using CC/GQDs in which the formed Hg(0) can be effectively used as an electrochemical sensor for NB.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version.

Jincymol Kappen: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Manikka Kubendran Aravind: Validation. Perumal Varalakshmi: Writing – review & editing. Balasubramaniem Ashokkumar: Writing – review & editing. S. A. John: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Department of Biotechnology (DBT), New Delhi, India for the financial support (BT/PR20469/BCE/8/1394/2016). We are very much grateful to Dr. Meenakshi, Department of Chemistry, GRI for providing ICP-AES facility.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.128542.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltammetric determination of ultratraces of albumin, cysteine, and cystine at the hanging mercury drop electrode

Gedanken, Sustainable Existence of Solid Mercury (Hg) at Room Temperature and their Applications, Chem. Sci. 12 (2021) 3226–3238, https://doi.org/10.1039/D0SC06139E.

Mater. 279 (2014) 117–124, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2014.06.066.

Photobiol. B: Biol. 170 (2017) 164–172, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2017.03.018.
